metadata.title: Strategic Global Trade Flows — Chanyuan Politics (产缘政治) Dashboard
metadata.description: Curated trade flows mapping supply chain control nodes, upstream monopolies, and chokepoint vulnerabilities. Structured around the chanyuan politics framework analyzing power through production network interdependence.

vulnerability_scale.critical: Importer has no near-term alternative; disruption causes systemic economic or security failure
vulnerability_scale.high: Alternatives exist but require 2-5 years to develop; significant economic damage on disruption
vulnerability_scale.medium: Multiple alternative suppliers available; adjustment costly but manageable within 1-2 years

properties.strategic_note: ASML holds a global monopoly on EUV lithography, the sole technology capable of producing sub-7nm chips. This is the single most important chokepoint in the global semiconductor supply chain — without Dutch EUV systems, TSMC cannot manufacture leading-edge processors.

Despite US export controls, China remains deeply dependent on Taiwanese foundry output for mid-range chips used in automotive, industrial, and consumer electronics. TSMC's dominance creates a structural vulnerability that Chinese domestic fabs cannot yet replace at scale.

US dependence on TSMC for advanced logic chips (Apple, Nvidia, AMD designs) represents a critical national security vulnerability. The CHIPS Act attempts to reshore production, but TSMC Arizona remains years behind Taiwan's leading nodes.

properties.commodity: EDA Software and Chip Design IP
properties.strategic_note: Synopsys, Cadence, and Siemens EDA (US-controlled) hold ~85% of the EDA market. Without American design software, no advanced chip can be designed anywhere. This is a less visible but equally potent upstream chokepoint controlled by US export regulations.

JSR, Tokyo Ohka, and Shin-Etsu dominate EUV photoresists and 300mm silicon wafers. Japanese materials expertise represents a quiet upstream monopoly — TSMC's fabs cannot operate without continuous supply of Japanese-origin consumables.

Samsung and SK Hynix supply the majority of China's DRAM and NAND needs for servers, smartphones, and data centers. China's YMTC and CXMT remain generations behind, making Korean memory a persistent dependency despite nationalist semiconductor ambitions.

Japan's 2019 export restrictions on three semiconductor materials to Korea demonstrated how upstream materials control can weaponize supply chains politically. Korea has since diversified but remains structurally dependent on Japanese chemical purity standards.

Samsung Foundry's competitiveness versus TSMC depends entirely on access to ASML's latest EUV tools. Any Dutch or US-coordinated restriction on ASML shipments to Korea would cripple Samsung's advanced node roadmap.

China dominates mature-node chip production (28nm+) and advanced packaging (OSAT). US attempts to restrict Chinese advanced chips inadvertently accelerate China's legacy chip dominance, creating a reverse dependency in automotive, industrial, and defense supply chains.

ZEISS SMT is the sole supplier of mirror optics for ASML EUV systems. This German monopoly sits one tier deeper in the supply chain than ASML itself — a hidden chokepoint that makes the semiconductor stack fundamentally European-American controlled at its root.

properties.commodity: Semiconductor Manufacturing Equipment (Applied Materials, Lam Research, KLA)
properties.strategic_note: US equipment makers supply critical etch, deposition, and inspection tools that complement ASML lithography. The US-Netherlands-Japan trilateral export control agreement coordinates restrictions on China through this interconnected equipment ecosystem.

SK Hynix supplies over 50% of global HBM used in Nvidia AI GPUs. As AI training scales exponentially, HBM has become the binding constraint on compute buildout — a new chokepoint emerging from the AI arms race between US and China.

German automakers (VW, BMW, Mercedes) increasingly depend on Chinese mature-node chips for vehicle electronics. The 2021 chip shortage exposed this vulnerability. China's state-subsidized expansion of legacy fabs creates a growing structural dependency for European industry.

US export controls restrict Nvidia/AMD AI chips to China, but compliance-variant products (A800, H800) and gray-market diversion maintain substantial flows. This is the sharpest edge of techno-economic warfare — an attempt to cap an adversary's AI capability through chanyuan chokepoint leverage.

China's growing dominance in advanced packaging substrates (ABF substrates, leadframes) creates a subtle reverse dependency for TSMC's CoWoS advanced packaging. This is often overlooked in semiconductor decoupling analysis.

Tokyo Electron and Screen Holdings supply critical coat/develop and etch tools for US fabs. Japan's role in the trilateral chip alliance (US-NL-JP) gives it quiet geopolitical leverage — it could theoretically defect from the China restriction coalition.

Vietnam's emerging role as alternative semiconductor assembly hub still depends heavily on Chinese component inputs, revealing how supply chain 'diversification' often creates new dependencies rather than eliminating them.

Despite joining US-led export controls in 2023, Japan still exports substantial non-restricted semiconductor equipment to China. Chinese fabs stockpiled Japanese tools before restrictions tightened, creating a time-limited window of domestic capability buildout.

China's largest single oil supplier. The Saudi-China energy relationship underpins Beijing's push for yuan-denominated oil trading. Saudi Arabia's willingness to accept yuan settlements directly challenges petrodollar hegemony — a structural shift in the global financial order.

Post-sanctions surge made Russia China's second-largest oil supplier. Discounted Russian crude gives China a structural energy cost advantage over Western competitors. The ESPO pipeline and Arctic shipping create non-Malacca supply routes — a geopolitical hedge.

Iraq is China's third-largest oil supplier, with Chinese NOCs holding major stakes in Iraqi oilfields. This creates a chanyuan lock-in: Chinese upstream investment secures downstream supply, while Iraqi infrastructure depends on Chinese capital and labor.

China purchases ~90% of Iran's oil exports through shadow fleets and ship-to-ship transfers, defying US secondary sanctions. This flow reveals the limits of financial weaponization when the buyer has sufficient economic mass to absorb enforcement risk.

UAE's Murban crude is a key China-bound grade. Abu Dhabi's petrochemical integration with Chinese refinery investment creates bilateral lock-in beyond simple commodity trade — a chanyuan politics archetype of mutual dependency embedding.

Japan imports ~95% of its oil, with Saudi Arabia as the top supplier. This extreme energy dependency has shaped Japanese foreign policy since the 1973 oil shock and constrains Tokyo's ability to align fully with Washington on Middle East policy.

South Korea's massive refining sector (Hyundai Oilbank, SK Energy, S-Oil — partly Saudi-owned) processes Saudi crude for re-export. Saudi Aramco's equity stake in Korean refineries is a chanyuan integration strategy — ownership along the value chain, not just commodity sales.

India's second-largest oil supplier. As India's economy grows toward $5T GDP, its oil import dependency deepens, making the Saudi-India energy corridor strategically important and giving Riyadh leverage over Delhi's foreign policy flexibility.

Post-Ukraine, European crude imports pivoted dramatically from Russia to US shale. This energy reorientation deepened European strategic dependence on the US, constraining European foreign policy autonomy — energy dependency as geopolitical leash.

India became Russia's largest seaborne crude buyer post-2022, purchasing heavily discounted Urals blend. Indian refiners profit from the discount and re-export refined products to Europe — a sanctions arbitrage that reveals the limits of Western financial coercion.

Norway replaced Russia as Europe's top pipeline oil supplier. As a NATO member but non-EU state, Norway occupies a unique structural position — reliable supply without Brussels governance, but also without EU solidarity obligations.

Angola's oil-for-infrastructure deals with China exemplify Beijing's resource-backed lending model across Africa. Chinese NOCs hold significant upstream stakes in Angolan blocks, embedding chanyuan control through capital-resource interlocking.

Brazil's pre-salt deepwater oil has made it a top-five global producer and key diversification source for China. Unlike Middle Eastern supply, Brazilian crude routes avoid the Strait of Hormuz chokepoint, though Malacca dependency remains.

Japan is the world's largest LNG importer post-Fukushima nuclear shutdown. Qatari long-term contracts provide supply security, but Japan's LNG dependency transits the Strait of Hormuz and Malacca Strait — two chokepoints simultaneously.

Korea Gas Corporation (KOGAS) holds large long-term contracts with Qatar. South Korea's LNG-heavy power generation mix makes this an energy security lifeline that shapes Seoul's Middle East diplomacy and naval presence.

China signed a 27-year LNG deal with Qatar in 2022, the longest in history. These ultra-long-term contracts lock in supply security but also constrain future flexibility — a conscious trade-off of optionality for certainty in energy planning.

US LNG exports to Europe surged 4x after Nord Stream sabotage. Germany built LNG terminals in record time, but US LNG is 30-50% more expensive than Russian pipeline gas. Europe traded energy cost competitiveness for geopolitical alignment — with permanent industrial implications.

Australia is Japan's largest LNG supplier, with routes avoiding Hormuz entirely. The Australia-Japan energy axis is a cornerstone of the Quad's economic architecture — strategic supply diversification embedded in a security alliance.

Despite deteriorating bilateral relations, China remains a major Australian LNG buyer. Canberra's diplomatic tensions with Beijing coexist with deep commodity interdependence — a textbook case of chanyuan politics constraining geopolitical realignment.

Norway became Europe's top pipeline gas supplier after Nord Stream destruction. The Langeled and Europipe systems now carry existential importance for German industry. Norway's structural position gives Oslo extraordinary quiet leverage over European energy policy.

China consumes ~73% of global seaborne iron ore, with Australia supplying ~60% of that. This is the world's single largest bilateral commodity dependency. BHP, Rio Tinto, and Fortescue are structurally irreplaceable at current Chinese steel production levels. Australia tried to weaponize this dependency during diplomatic tensions but found the dependency was mutual.

Vale is China's second-largest iron ore supplier after Australian miners. Longer shipping distances (40+ days vs. 14 for Australia) create a logistics cost premium, but Vale provides essential diversification. China has invested in the Simandou mine (Guinea) to further reduce Australian dependence.

Chile produces ~25% of global copper, critical for electrification and EV wiring. China's copper smelting dominance means Chilean ore is processed in China, creating a chanyuan midstream control point. Chile cannot easily bypass Chinese smelters for its own value-added processing.

DRC produces ~74% of global cobalt, essential for lithium-ion batteries. Chinese firms (CMOC, Huayou Cobalt) own or control ~70% of DRC cobalt mining operations. This is the most concentrated upstream-to-midstream control in any critical mineral supply chain — a textbook chanyuan monopoly.

Indonesia banned raw nickel ore exports in 2020, forcing downstream processing domestically — largely by Chinese-owned smelters (Tsingshan). This is Indonesia's attempt at industrial upgrading via resource nationalism, but Chinese capital controls the processing infrastructure.

Australia mines ~47% of global lithium, but China processes ~65% of it into battery-grade lithium hydroxide and carbonate. The upstream extraction vs. midstream processing split exemplifies how resource richness does not guarantee value chain control — refining is the chokepoint.

properties.commodity: Manganese Ore and Platinum Group Metals
properties.strategic_note: South Africa holds 75% of global manganese reserves (essential for steel) and 70% of platinum reserves (catalytic converters, hydrogen fuel cells). Chinese smelters process the bulk of South African manganese — another extraction-processing split where China captures midstream value.

Peru is the world's second-largest copper producer. Chinese mining firms (MMG, Chinalco) own major Peruvian copper operations. Domestic political instability and community opposition to mining create supply disruption risk that cascades through global copper markets.

Guinea holds the world's largest bauxite reserves and supplies over half of China's bauxite imports for aluminum production. Chinese consortiums (Winning International) built rail and port infrastructure to extract bauxite — resource-infrastructure bundling as chanyuan penetration strategy.

South Korea's battery giants (LG, Samsung SDI, SK On) source lithium from Chile's Atacama Desert to bypass Chinese processing. This represents a conscious attempt to build a non-China critical mineral pathway for the EV supply chain.

Korean battery makers attempt direct DRC cobalt sourcing to reduce reliance on Chinese-controlled cobalt refining. However, Chinese firms own the majority of DRC mining operations, making genuine supply chain independence extremely difficult.

Japan's EV battery supply chain depends on Indonesian nickel processed in Chinese-invested smelters. Japan faces a strategic dilemma: access to critical nickel requires engaging with Chinese-controlled processing infrastructure in a third country.

India's steel industry depends heavily on Australian coking coal. As India pursues infrastructure-led growth, this dependency deepens — linking Australian resource extraction to Indian industrialization ambitions through a durable structural channel.

China's 2010 rare earth embargo against Japan remains the canonical example of supply chain weaponization. Japan has since diversified (Lynas, deep-sea deposits) but China still controls ~70% of global processing. Rare earth magnets are essential for EVs, wind turbines, and precision-guided munitions.

The US defense sector depends on Chinese NdFeB magnets for F-35 components, Tomahawk missiles, and satellite systems. This is arguably the most strategically dangerous single-source dependency in the US military-industrial complex — a vulnerability Beijing could exploit in a Taiwan contingency.

German automakers need rare earth magnets for EV motors. As the EU mandates electrification by 2035, European rare earth dependency on China deepens structurally — the green transition creates new geopolitical vulnerabilities even as it addresses climate ones.

Lynas (Australia) is the only non-Chinese rare earth mine-to-magnet operation at scale, but China still processes significant Australian rare earth concentrate. Australia's attempt to build sovereign processing capacity faces Chinese cost competition and technical barriers.

Myanmar's Kachin State supplies ~50% of China's heavy rare earth feedstock (dysprosium, terbium — critical for high-temperature magnets). Civil conflict and ethnic militia control of mining areas make this supply chain uniquely fragile and opaque.

China imports ~100 million tonnes of soybeans annually, with Brazil supplying ~65%. This is the largest agricultural trade flow globally. Chinese crushing plants and animal feed depend on Brazilian soy — food security through external agricultural land use, a neo-colonial pattern reconfigured through market mechanisms.

The 2018 trade war demonstrated how China weaponized soybean purchases, redirecting from US to Brazilian suppliers. US farmers suffered, but China's shift proved that food imports can be redirected faster than industrial supply chains — a key lesson in commodity fungibility vs. technological specificity.

Ukraine was Egypt's top wheat supplier before the 2022 invasion. Disruption triggered bread price spikes across the Middle East and North Africa, demonstrating how food supply chain shocks in one region cascade into political instability in another — the 2011 Arab Spring was partly triggered by similar dynamics.

Argentina completes the soybean triad (Brazil-US-Argentina) that feeds Chinese protein demand. Argentine export taxes and currency controls create periodic supply disruptions, but China's strategic grain reserves buffer against short-term shocks.

China imposed 80% tariffs on Australian barley in 2020 as economic coercion during diplomatic tensions, then reversed them in 2023. This demonstrated both the possibility and limits of food trade weaponization — substitution was costly but achievable for both parties.

Japan's caloric self-sufficiency is only ~38%, with the US as the dominant grain supplier. This food dependency reinforces the US-Japan security alliance at a structural level — Japan cannot sustain its population without American agricultural exports.

EU-Mercosur trade tensions center on Brazilian beef and soy imports driving Amazonian deforestation. European agricultural lobbies resist Brazilian competition while EU industry wants Mercosur market access — a classic internal chanyuan tension between sectoral interests.

Thailand and Vietnam together supply most of Asia's rice trade. India's periodic rice export bans (2023) demonstrated how food nationalism in one major producer cascades into price spikes affecting 3 billion rice-dependent consumers across Asia and Africa.

China blocked Canadian canola imports in 2019 during the Meng Wanzhou arrest crisis, demonstrating agricultural trade as a diplomatic coercion tool. Canada's concentrated export dependence on China for canola creates asymmetric vulnerability in bilateral disputes.

properties.commodity: Grain (alternative corridor via EU Solidarity Lanes)
properties.strategic_note: The Black Sea grain corridor's collapse forced Ukrainian grain through overland EU routes, disrupting Polish and Baltic agricultural markets. This demonstrated how war-induced supply chain rerouting creates new domestic political tensions in transit states.

properties.commodity: Machine Tools and Precision Manufacturing Equipment
properties.strategic_note: German Mittelstand companies supply precision machine tools that Chinese manufacturers cannot yet replicate. TRUMPF lasers, DMG Mori machining centers, and Siemens industrial automation form the backbone of Chinese high-end manufacturing. This dependency is an underappreciated lever for European China policy.

Fanuc, Yaskawa, and Mitsubishi Electric robots automate Chinese factories. Japan's industrial robotics dominance is declining as Chinese firms (SIASUN, Estun) improve, but Japanese precision still leads in automotive and aerospace applications. A slow-motion technology transfer undermines Japan's structural advantage.

The single largest bilateral manufactured goods flow globally. US consumers depend on Chinese assembly of Apple, Dell, HP products. While final assembly is low value-added, the production network lock-in (supplier ecosystems, logistics infrastructure, worker skill concentration) makes rapid reshoring structurally infeasible.

Chinese EV and solar panel exports to Europe tripled 2021-2024, triggering EU anti-subsidy tariffs. This is a chanyuan politics inflection point: Chinese state-subsidized overcapacity in green technology threatens European industrial champions while accelerating European decarbonization. The EU faces an irresolvable tension between climate goals and industrial sovereignty.

Hyundai, Kia, and Korean battery makers (LG, SK On, Samsung SDI) are building massive US production capacity under IRA incentives. This represents the most successful case of US industrial policy pulling allied manufacturing onshore — chanyuan realignment through subsidy rather than tariff.

Toyota, Honda, and Nissan have deeply localized US production since the 1980s voluntary export restraints. Japanese auto FDI in the US South created political constituencies that defend free trade — a case study in how chanyuan integration can reshape domestic politics in the importer country.

Vietnam is the primary beneficiary of China+1 supply chain diversification, especially for Samsung and Apple assembly. However, Vietnamese factories depend heavily on Chinese-supplied components, making 'diversification' partially illusory — the production shifted, but the supply chain didn't.

Mexico surpassed China as the US's largest trade partner in 2023. USMCA-compliant auto production near-shores supply chains, but Chinese firms (BYD, CATL) are building Mexican plants to access the US market duty-free — China routing production through Mexico to circumvent US tariffs. The chanyuan network adapts around trade barriers.

The German-US manufacturing corridor is the backbone of the transatlantic economic relationship. BMW's Spartanburg plant is the largest US vehicle exporter. German pharma (Bayer, BASF) supplies critical inputs. Trump-era tariff threats exposed how this bilateral dependency constrains European strategic autonomy.

India supplies ~40% of US generic drugs and is a critical pharmaceutical API source. COVID exposed how US drug supply chains depend on Indian generics manufacturers, who in turn depend on Chinese API (active pharmaceutical ingredient) inputs — a cascading vulnerability chain.

properties.commodity: Infrastructure Equipment, Consumer Goods, and Machinery
properties.strategic_note: China-Africa manufactured goods trade is deeply asymmetric: Africa exports raw materials and imports finished products — a 21st-century version of colonial trade patterns. Chinese construction companies bundle infrastructure loans with Chinese labor and equipment, creating long-term structural dependency.

Taiwanese firms (Foxconn, Pegatron, Wistron) are shifting assembly from China to Vietnam, driven by US tariff avoidance and geopolitical hedging. Taiwan's FDI in Vietnam creates a parallel production network outside Chinese control — a deliberate chanyuan risk diversification strategy.

Brazil's trade with China mirrors the classic raw materials-for-manufactures pattern: Brazil exports soybeans, iron ore, and oil; China exports electronics, machinery, and textiles. Brazilian industry struggles to compete with Chinese manufacturing, creating deindustrialization pressures reminiscent of the 'resource curse' dynamic.

India's generic pharma industry — which supplies 20% of global generic drugs — depends on Chinese APIs for ~68% of its inputs. This creates a hidden chokepoint: China controls the upstream chemistry that enables India's downstream pharmaceutical exports to the world.

Mexican electronics manufacturing (Foxconn Juarez, Samsung Tijuana) grows rapidly as nearshoring accelerates. However, maquiladora electronics still rely heavily on Asian component imports, raising questions about whether nearshoring truly reduces strategic vulnerability or merely relocates final assembly.

The US-Saudi arms relationship is the foundation of the petrodollar system and Persian Gulf security architecture. Saudi dependence on US spare parts and maintenance creates long-term lock-in — weapons systems as 30-year geopolitical subscription contracts.

US arms sales to Taiwan are the most politically sensitive defense trade globally, directly testing Chinese red lines. Taiwan's shift toward asymmetric defense (sea mines, mobile missiles) reflects acceptance that conventional force parity with the PLA is unachievable — deterrence through cost imposition rather than denial.

India's military runs predominantly on Russian platforms, creating a decades-long maintenance dependency. Delhi's attempt to diversify (French Rafale, US MQ-9B) faces compatibility constraints with its Russian-origin fleet. CAATSA sanctions on S-400 purchases test the limits of US tolerance for Indian strategic autonomy.

properties.commodity: Rafale Fighter Jets and Naval Systems
properties.strategic_note: France's Rafale sale to India represents European defense industry's push into the Indo-Pacific market, challenging US and Russian dominance. Dassault's willingness to transfer technology exceeds US practice, making France a preferred diversification partner for nations seeking to reduce single-source dependency.

properties.commodity: Defense Equipment (JF-17 Aircraft, Naval Vessels, Missiles)
properties.strategic_note: China-Pakistan defense cooperation is the deepest bilateral arms relationship outside the US alliance system. Joint production of JF-17 fighters and ongoing naval programs (Type 054A frigates) create a chanyuan lock-in where Pakistani military doctrine and logistics are permanently calibrated to Chinese systems.

Japan's historic defense spending increase to 2% GDP primarily benefits US defense contractors. F-35 procurement, Tomahawk cruise missiles, and integrated Aegis systems deepen interoperability to the point where the JSDF cannot operate independently of US C4ISR networks — alliance as structural dependency by design.

China controls ~80% of global solar panel manufacturing. US anti-dumping tariffs led to Chinese production rerouting through Southeast Asia (Cambodia, Vietnam, Thailand). The Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act adds ethical screening complexity. Green transition goals and supply chain security goals are in direct tension.

Chinese EV and battery firms (BYD, CATL, Gotion) investing in Mexico to access the US market via USMCA — the most strategically consequential tariff arbitrage in global trade. If successful, Chinese firms embed themselves in the North American production network, making future decoupling vastly more difficult.

properties.commodity: Nickel Smelting FDI and Industrial Equipment
properties.strategic_note: Chinese investment in Indonesian nickel processing (Tsingshan's Morowali industrial park) creates a template for resource-state industrial upgrading: Indonesia gains downstream processing but Chinese capital controls the technology, infrastructure, and offtake agreements. Sovereignty over processing is contested.

US THAAD deployment in South Korea triggered Chinese economic retaliation against Korean companies (Lotte, Hyundai), demonstrating how a third party's defense procurement decisions cascade into bilateral economic punishment. Korea occupies the most structurally contested position in the US-China chanyuan competition.

As German firms hedge against China dependency, India emerges as an alternative market for German capital goods. The EU-India FTA negotiations aim to deepen this corridor — a deliberate chanyuan rebalancing from overdependence on Chinese industrial demand.

BYD and Great Wall Motor invested heavily in Thai EV plants, displacing Japanese automakers' decades-long dominance. Thailand's pivot from Japan's auto manufacturing hub to China's EV assembly hub illustrates how technological disruption reshuffles chanyuan alignments — the ICE-to-EV transition is simultaneously an industrial geography revolution.

properties.commodity: Nuclear Fuel Components and Critical Minerals for Energy
properties.strategic_note: France's nuclear energy independence (70% of electricity) still depends on imported uranium processing and specialized components, with China increasingly present in the nuclear fuel supply chain. France's celebrated energy sovereignty has hidden upstream dependencies.

Saudi Aramco's petrochemical integration with Chinese refineries (SABIC-Sinopec joint ventures) goes beyond crude supply into value-chain embedding. Aramco's $10B investment in a Fujian refinery complex creates bilateral lock-in at the processing stage — chanyuan integration deeper than simple commodity trade.

properties.commodity: Lithium Mining FDI and Processing Equipment
properties.strategic_note: Chinese firms (Ganfeng, Zijin) hold major stakes in Argentina's lithium triangle operations. Milei's market-friendly policies attract more Chinese lithium investment, creating an ironic dynamic: a libertarian, pro-US president deepening resource dependency on China through market logic.

European rearmament post-Ukraine overwhelmingly benefits US defense contractors over European ones (Rheinmetall, KNDS, Leonardo). The 2% GDP defense spending commitment channels European taxpayer money to American firms, deepening transatlantic defense-industrial dependency at the very moment Europe seeks strategic autonomy.

Korean battery makers (LG, Samsung SDI, SK On) source ~70% of cathode and anode materials from China. Despite Korean dominance in battery cell manufacturing, Chinese control of precursor materials creates an upstream vulnerability that IRA 'foreign entity of concern' rules attempt to address through forced supply chain restructuring.

BYD's $1B Turkish EV plant circumvents EU tariffs on Chinese EVs — Turkey's customs union with the EU creates a backdoor for Chinese automotive exports into Europe. Ankara leverages its geographic position between trade blocs to attract manufacturing investment from both sides.

US reshoring of semiconductor fabrication and EV manufacturing dramatically increases domestic copper demand. Securing non-Chinese copper supply chains from Chile and Peru is becoming a US national security priority, reflected in the Minerals Security Partnership.

The EU-Vietnam FTA (2020) enables Vietnam as a China+1 manufacturing alternative for European supply chains. Samsung's massive Vietnamese production complex exports primarily to EU markets, creating a Korean-managed, Vietnamese-based production network that partially bypasses Chinese assembly.

Japan's automotive electrification depends heavily on Chinese battery materials despite Toyota and Panasonic's efforts to build alternative supply chains. Japan's traditional automotive supply chain advantage erodes as the industry shifts from mechanical to electrochemical — a chanyuan disruption driven by technological transition.

India is the 'pharmacy of the Global South,' supplying affordable generics to Africa and Southeast Asia. This creates a distinctive chanyuan position: India's pharmaceutical diplomacy builds soft power and structural dependence across developing nations, paralleling China's infrastructure model but in healthcare.

Australia supplies nearly half the world's lithium ore, but China refines over 65% into battery-grade compounds. This upstream-midstream split is the defining chanyuan asymmetry of the clean energy transition: resource abundance confers less sovereignty than processing capacity, and Beijing has systematically built the refining chokepoint that converts Australian rocks into EV-grade lithium hydroxide.

Chile's Atacama evaporation ponds yield high-purity lithium carbonate sent overwhelmingly to Chinese cathode producers. SQM and Albemarle operate extraction, but Chinese firms increasingly secure offtake agreements. Santiago's new state lithium company signals resource nationalism, yet downstream processing remains firmly in Chinese hands — a structural dependency that resource sovereignty rhetoric cannot overcome without industrial policy.

Argentina's lithium triangle hosts massive brine deposits where Chinese firms (Ganfeng, Zijin) have secured controlling stakes. Milei's deregulation accelerates FDI inflows, deepening the chanyuan paradox: a libertarian, pro-Washington president presiding over structural resource integration with Beijing, because market logic and Chinese capital availability outweigh geopolitical alignment preferences.

DRC produces 70% of global cobalt, with Glencore and CMOC (China Molybdenum) dominating extraction. Chinese firms control the refining bottleneck, processing over 80% of cobalt into battery-grade material. This dual chokepoint — extraction concentration plus refining dominance — makes cobalt the most structurally vulnerable input in the EV supply chain, with artisanal mining adding ESG risk that Western OEMs struggle to manage.

Indonesia's nickel ore export ban forced downstream processing onshore, but Chinese smelter FDI dominates the industrial parks (Morowali, Weda Bay). Jakarta captured value-add through resource nationalism, yet Chinese capital and technology control the smelting chokepoint. This is chanyuan politics in action: sovereignty over the resource does not equal sovereignty over the production network when FDI determines processing capacity.

China commands over 80% of global solar panel production across the entire value chain — polysilicon, wafers, cells, and modules. European decarbonization targets create structural demand that no alternative supplier can fill at scale. The EU's anti-subsidy investigation confronts an impossible trilemma: industrial sovereignty, climate targets, and consumer energy costs cannot all be optimized simultaneously when the production network is this concentrated.

US anti-dumping tariffs and UFLPA forced Chinese solar production to reroute through Southeast Asian intermediaries (Cambodia, Vietnam, Thailand), but the underlying production network remains Chinese-controlled. The IRA's domestic manufacturing incentives are building alternative capacity, yet reaching cost parity with Chinese scale remains years away — revealing the gap between legislative intent and production network reality.

CATL and BYD supply batteries to virtually every European automaker, creating a dependency that threatens the continent's automotive industrial base — the sector that anchors German, French, and Italian manufacturing employment. CATL's Hungarian gigafactory represents the next phase: embedding Chinese battery production inside the EU's regulatory perimeter, a chanyuan strategy of territorial integration that tariffs cannot easily counter.

IRA's 'foreign entity of concern' rules aim to exclude Chinese battery content from EV tax credits by 2025-2027, forcing automakers to restructure supply chains. Yet CATL's technology licensing to Ford's Michigan plant reveals the limits of decoupling: you can ban the entity but not the knowledge. The US faces a chanyuan dilemma where EV adoption speed and supply chain independence are inversely correlated.

Japan's delayed EV transition (Toyota's hydrogen bet) has not insulated it from Chinese battery material dependency. As Japanese automakers belatedly electrify, their cathode and anode material sourcing runs overwhelmingly through Chinese processors. Panasonic's partnership with Tesla drives demand, but the upstream chanyuan position belongs to Chinese chemical firms who control precursor supply.

German precision engineering in gearboxes and bearings (ZF, Schaeffler) remains essential for high-capacity wind turbines, even as Chinese turbine manufacturers (Goldwind, Envision) dominate unit volumes. This niche illustrates a residual European chanyuan advantage in precision components — but Chinese domestic substitution programs threaten to erode it within a decade.

China's wind turbine exports to the Global South — Brazil's northeast coast being the largest single market — represent a new vector of energy infrastructure dependency. As Goldwind and Envision undercut Vestas and Siemens Gamesa on price, developing nations lock into Chinese maintenance ecosystems and technical standards, extending Beijing's chanyuan influence through renewable energy infrastructure rather than fossil fuel supply.

Korean battery giants (LG Energy Solution, Samsung SDI, SK On) are the IRA's intended beneficiaries — allied-nation suppliers who can replace Chinese batteries for US EV tax credit eligibility. Their Georgia and Tennessee gigafactories represent the physical manifestation of friend-shoring policy. Yet Korean firms remain dependent on Chinese cathode precursors, creating a hidden chanyuan vulnerability one tier upstream from the 'allied' production.

India's ambitious solar targets (500 GW by 2030) collide with near-total dependence on Chinese cells and modules. Delhi's PLI scheme for domestic solar manufacturing has attracted investment, but achieving cost parity with Chinese scale remains elusive. India faces the sharpest version of the green sovereignty dilemma: the fastest path to energy independence (solar) runs through dependence on a strategic rival's production network.

Australia-Korea lithium trade represents a deliberate attempt to build a non-China critical mineral pathway for EV batteries. Korean firms invest directly in Australian mines (POSCO's Pilgangoora stake) to secure supply outside Chinese refining chokepoints. This 'allied mineral corridor' is the material foundation of friend-shoring — but its viability depends on Korean firms building refining capacity that currently does not exist at Chinese scale.

properties.commodity: AI GPU Chips (TSMC fab for Nvidia/AMD)
properties.strategic_note: TSMC fabricates virtually all cutting-edge AI accelerators (Nvidia H100/B200, AMD MI300X) at 3-5nm nodes in Hsinchu. This single-island concentration of AI compute manufacturing is the most consequential chanyuan chokepoint of the 2020s. The Arizona fab diversifies geography but not control — TSMC's process knowledge cannot be replicated by fiat. A Taiwan contingency would crater global AI development capacity overnight.

SK Hynix commands 90%+ of the HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) market essential for AI training accelerators. Each Nvidia H100 requires six HBM3 stacks; each B200 requires more. This Korean duopoly (with Samsung as a distant second) is the memory chokepoint of the AI revolution — without HBM, the most advanced GPUs are silicon paperweights. US export controls on HBM to China weaponize this allied-nation production network.

properties.commodity: EUV Lithography for AI Chip Production
properties.strategic_note: ASML's EUV machines are the upstream chokepoint of AI compute fabrication — without them, sub-7nm AI chips cannot be manufactured. Each machine costs $200M+ and takes 18 months to build. This Dutch-Taiwanese axis is the most concentrated production dependency in the AI stack: one company, one buyer for the highest-end tools, manufacturing chips for one dominant designer (Nvidia). The chanyuan leverage is absolute at this node.

US-designed AI accelerators flow to allied-nation data centers, with the UK as a major hub for AI research and deployment (DeepMind, ARM ecosystem). Nvidia's export licensing regime creates a de facto US-controlled allocation system for global AI compute capacity. The intelligence-industrial complex dimension is stark: access to frontier AI chips determines which nations can train sovereign foundation models, making chip allocation a tool of techno-hegemonic influence.

The Nvidia H20 — a compliance-variant chip designed to skirt US export control thresholds — embodies the cat-and-mouse dynamic of techno-economic containment. Each regulatory tightening prompts a redesigned chip that maximizes performance within the new limits. This flow reveals the fundamental tension in export controls: strict enough to degrade Chinese AI capability, but permissive enough to preserve Nvidia's revenue. The chokepoint is real but leaky by design.

Huawei's Ascend chips represent China's indigenous AI compute ambition, fabricated at SMIC's 7nm node without EUV. While performance lags Nvidia's frontier chips by a generation, the Ascend 910B serves inference workloads adequately. Limited exports to price-sensitive markets test whether Chinese AI silicon can establish a parallel ecosystem. This is the counterfactual to US chokepoint strategy: can denial of frontier tools accelerate rather than prevent indigenous capability development?

Malaysia's Penang corridor hosts critical OSAT (Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test) facilities that package AI chips after TSMC fabrication. CoWoS and other advanced packaging for AI GPUs increasingly bottleneck production — TSMC's own CoWoS capacity cannot meet demand. Malaysian packaging thus becomes a second chokepoint in the AI compute supply chain, less visible than fabrication but equally essential for translating silicon into functional AI accelerators.

Japanese materials firms (Ajinomoto Build-up Film, Ibiden substrates, Shin-Etsu wafers) supply critical packaging substrates for TSMC's AI chip production. These low-profile inputs are essential for CoWoS advanced packaging that enables HBM stacking on AI GPUs. Japan's materials chokepoint is less dramatic than ASML's EUV monopoly but equally difficult to substitute, representing the kind of deep production network knowledge that decades of specialization accumulate.

Ireland hosts major data center clusters for US hyperscalers (AWS, Microsoft, Google) serving European AI workloads, and exports networking equipment, power management systems, and server components. Dublin's favorable tax regime and EU market access make it the bridgehead of American AI infrastructure in Europe. This flow represents the physical layer of the intelligence-industrial complex — sovereign AI capability requires not just chips but the data center ecosystem to deploy them.

Norwegian hydroelectric power (cheap, carbon-free, abundant) attracts AI data center investment from hyperscalers seeking to meet sustainability pledges while scaling compute. The NordLink subsea cable exports surplus renewable energy to continental Europe's data center corridor. As AI training runs consume city-scale electricity, access to clean power becomes a locational determinant for AI infrastructure — Norway's geography (cold climate for cooling, hydroelectric abundance) constitutes a natural advantage in the emerging AI energy geography.

properties.commodity: North Sea Crude Oil and Natural Gas
properties.strategic_note: UK's North Sea production declining, increasing reliance on Norwegian pipeline gas and crude. Post-Brexit energy security depends on bilateral Norway relationship outside EU frameworks.
UK banned Huawei from 5G networks but remains dependent on Chinese consumer electronics and components. Decoupling rhetoric exceeds structural reality.
US LNG exports to UK surged post-2022 as European energy security realigned toward Atlantic supply. UK serves as LNG import hub for Northwestern Europe.
UK pharma industry (AstraZeneca, GSK) provides critical vaccine and drug exports. Post-COVID this represents strategic leverage in health security.

properties.commodity: Rare Earth Magnets for Defense and EVs
properties.strategic_note: UK defense industry (BAE Systems, Rolls-Royce) depends on Chinese rare earth permanent magnets for jet engines and precision-guided munitions.
Italy's industrial base increasingly depends on Chinese intermediate goods. Despite being first G7 to join BRI (later withdrawn), trade dependency deepened structurally.
Canada is a major agricultural exporter to China. Beijing weaponized canola imports during the Meng Wanzhou dispute (2018-2021), demonstrating how food trade becomes geopolitical leverage.

properties.commodity: Automobiles and Auto Parts (integrated supply chain)
properties.strategic_note: US-Canada auto supply chain is the most integrated bilateral manufacturing relationship globally. Parts cross the border 6-8 times during assembly. USMCA tariff disruption would collapse both countries' auto sectors.
Canada is the largest foreign oil supplier to the US ($80B/yr). Pipeline infrastructure (Keystone, Enbridge Line 5) makes this a captive bilateral relationship — neither party can easily redirect.
France's digital infrastructure depends on Chinese equipment despite EU screening mechanisms. Huawei restrictions applied selectively, leaving significant component-level dependency.
France diversified from Russian gas via US LNG imports through Dunkirk and Fos-sur-Mer terminals. Nuclear power (70% of electricity) provides energy security buffer that most EU states lack.

properties.commodity: Rare Earths for Aerospace and Defense
properties.strategic_note: Safran, Thales, and Dassault depend on Chinese rare earth magnets for jet engines, radar systems, and guided munitions. French defense autonomy is structurally constrained by this dependency.

Nord Stream 1 (Vyborg→Lubmin, 1222km subsea) destroyed by sabotage in September 2022. Previously carried 55 bcm/yr — Germany's primary Russian gas artery. Destruction forced emergency LNG pivot costing €100B+. THE defining infrastructure attack of the current era.

Nord Stream 2 completed but never commercially operated due to German regulatory freeze after Feb 2022 invasion. Destroyed alongside NS1. $11B investment rendered worthless — the most expensive geopolitical stranded asset in history.

TurkStream (Anapa→Kıyıköy, 930km subsea) became Russia's primary gas export route to Europe after Nord Stream destruction. Turkey leverages transit position for geopolitical concessions. Gas continues to Serbia, Hungary, Austria.

properties.commodity: Power of Siberia Gas Pipeline (active, 38 bcm/yr)
properties.strategic_note: Power of Siberia (Chayanda→Shanghai, 4000km) is Russia's pivot-to-Asia gas artery. Pipeline creates structural bilateral lock-in: neither party can redirect fixed infrastructure. Power of Siberia 2 via Mongolia under negotiation for additional 50 bcm/yr.

properties.commodity: TransMed Gas Pipeline (active, Algeria→Italy via Tunisia)
properties.strategic_note: TransMed (2475km, Hassi R'Mel→Sicily→mainland Italy) is Italy's largest gas supply after Russia reduction. Algeria replaced Russia as Italy's #1 supplier. Pipeline transits Tunisia, creating exposure to Sahel instability.

Southern Gas Corridor (TANAP+TAP, 3500km total) provides non-Russian gas to SE Europe. Expanded post-2022 from 10→20 bcm/yr. Azerbaijan positioned as EU's 'reliable alternative' despite authoritarian governance — a revealed preference test for EU values vs. energy security.

Druzhba ('Friendship') pipeline, world's longest oil pipeline network. Northern branch (via Belarus/Poland to Germany) halted by EU sanctions. Southern branch (via Ukraine to Hungary/Czech/Slovakia) continues under exemption — EU sanctions have carve-outs for landlocked states.

Yamal-Europe (4107km, Yamal→Belarus→Poland→Germany) halted by Russian counter-sanctions in 2022. Previously carried 33 bcm/yr. Poland refused to pay in rubles. Pipeline intact but politically frozen — could theoretically restart, unlike destroyed Nord Stream.

Russia-Ukraine gas transit contract expired January 1, 2025. Ukraine refused to renew, cutting off remaining Russian gas flow to Austria/Slovakia. This ended the last major Russian gas route to Central Europe — a structural decoupling made irreversible by war.

China refines 80% of global cobalt. SK and LG battery plants cannot produce cathodes without Chinese cobalt sulfate. This midstream processing monopoly gives Beijing leverage over the entire global EV supply chain regardless of where cobalt is mined.
US battery gigafactories (Tesla, GM Ultium, Ford BlueOval) depend on Chinese refined cobalt. IRA subsidies incentivize domestic processing but capacity won't match China's before 2030.

China processes 60% of global lithium into battery-grade chemicals. Even when Australia/Chile mine lithium, it ships to China for refining before reaching Korean battery makers. The processing chokepoint matters more than the mining location.
German automakers (VW, BMW, Mercedes) source battery-grade lithium chemicals from China. European lithium refining capacity (planned in Portugal, Germany) remains years from scale.

China produces 95% of global solar-grade polysilicon and 97% of wafers. US solar deployment depends entirely on Chinese upstream processing. Xinjiang forced labor sanctions created supply disruption but no alternative supply materialized.

India's solar ambitions (500GW by 2030) are structurally dependent on Chinese wafers and cells. Despite PLI subsidies for domestic manufacturing, Indian solar module assembly remains Chinese-component-dependent — classic assembly vs. manufacturing distinction.

properties.commodity: Synthetic and Natural Graphite Anode Material
properties.strategic_note: China controls 100% of global battery-grade graphite anode processing. Japan's Panasonic (Tesla supplier) has zero alternative source. Beijing restricted graphite exports in Oct 2023 as retaliation for US chip controls — demonstrating willingness to weaponize this monopoly.

properties.commodity: Graphite Anode Material for EV Batteries
properties.strategic_note: Every EV battery requires graphite anode — China processes 100%. US has zero domestic capacity. IRA requires non-China sourcing by 2025 but physically impossible to comply without losing EV tax credits or stopping production.
China smelts 50% of global copper and dominates copper foil for EV batteries and PCBs. South Korea's Samsung/SK electronics and battery production depends on Chinese refined copper products.

China controls 98% of gallium and 60% of germanium production — critical for radar, fiber optics, and compound semiconductors. Beijing restricted exports in Jul 2023, directly retaliating for US chip export controls. Small trade value but existential for defense supply chains.

properties.commodity: Gallium for Semiconductor and LED Industry
properties.strategic_note: Germany's Infineon and other European chipmakers depend on Chinese gallium for compound semiconductors. Export restrictions since 2023 created panic stockpiling across EU industry.
